coredata.dc:title: Development, characterization and application of chitosan/locust bean gum based multifunctional green food packaging containing Koelreuteria paniculata Laxm. bracts extract and Ti-carbon dots

coredata.dc:description: Multifunctional green food packaging films were developed by incorporating Koelreuteria paniculata Laxm. bract extract (KBE) and bio-waste-derived Ti-doped carbon dots (Ti-CDs) into a chitosan/locust bean gum (CG) matrix for the first time. Results from FTIR and XRD demonstrated the precise bonding of Ti-CDs to CG through a Schiff base reaction and hydrogen bonding, while KBE was effectively immobilized within the film matrix via hydrogen bonding. SEM and TGA analysis demonstrated enhanced thermal stability and density of the films. Addition of Ti-CDs synergistically improved the barrier properties and mechanical strength of the films through enhanced hydrogen bonding and Schiff base reactions. Specifically, the incorporation of 3 wt% Ti-CDs increased the oxygen barrier properties, tensile strength, water resistance, and vapor permeability of CG films by approximately 1.18, 0.75, and 1.51 times, respectively. Furthermore, the antimicrobial and antioxidant capabilities were significantly improved with the addition of KBE to films. The CG-3%CDs-KBE film coating effectively prolonged the shelf life of strawberries. Additionally, these films exhibited superior pH responsiveness and ammonia-sensitivity, enabling visual monitoring of shrimp freshness during storage. Importantly, CG-3%CDs-KBE films exhibited biodegradability in soil and displayed good biosafety. Overall, these findings underscore the promising potential of CG-3%CDs-KBE films as multifunctional green food packaging materials.

head.ce:title: Development, characterization and application of chitosan/locust bean gum based multifunctional green food packaging containing Koelreuteria paniculata Laxm. bracts extract and Ti-carbon dots

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Multifunctional green food packaging films were developed by incorporating Koelreuteria paniculata Laxm. bract extract (KBE) and bio-waste-derived Ti-doped carbon dots (Ti-CDs) into a chitosan/locust bean gum (CG) matrix for the first time. Results from FTIR and XRD demonstrated the precise bonding of Ti-CDs to CG through a Schiff base reaction and hydrogen bonding, while KBE was effectively immobilized within the film matrix via hydrogen bonding. SEM and TGA analysis demonstrated enhanced thermal stability and density of the films. Addition of Ti-CDs synergistically improved the barrier properties and mechanical strength of the films through enhanced hydrogen bonding and Schiff base reactions. Specifically, the incorporation of 3 wt% Ti-CDs increased the oxygen barrier properties, tensile strength, water resistance, and vapor permeability of CG films by approximately 1.18, 0.75, and 1.51 times, respectively. Furthermore, the antimicrobial and antioxidant capabilities were significantly improved with the addition of KBE to films. The CG-3%CDs-KBE film coating effectively prolonged the shelf life of strawberries. Additionally, these films exhibited superior pH responsiveness and ammonia-sensitivity, enabling visual monitoring of shrimp freshness during storage. Importantly, CG-3%CDs-KBE films exhibited biodegradability in soil and displayed good biosafety. Overall, these findings underscore the promising potential of CG-3%CDs-KBE films as multifunctional green food packaging materials.

ce:section.ce:para: Climate change is one of the most significant issues of our time, posing a major threat to humanity that needs to be addressed at multiple levels to mitigate global warming [1]. Plastic waste significantly contributes to greenhouse gas emissions and environmental pollution [2]. The increasing use of petroleum-based plastics has led to the accumulation of non-biodegradable waste, causing severe environmental contamination [3]. It is essential to embrace sustainable industrial practices, shift towards greener processes [4,5], and leverage agricultural by-products [6], such as those from farming and fruit waste, to curtail landfilling and control the emission of greenhouse gases [7–9]. The heightened public consciousness regarding environmental pollution, climate change, and the safety and quality of food has driven the food industry's interest in developing biodegradable active packaging films as substitutes for conventional plastics [10]. People are increasingly interested in developing new packaging materials derived from proteins (such as gelatin, soy protein isolate, and whey protein isolate), and natural polysaccharides (such as sodium alginate, starch, carrageenan, pectin and chitosan). These materials, derived from abundant sources, are gradually emerging as replacements for petroleum-based synthetic plastics. Especially, chitosan (CS), the second most abundant polysaccharide in nature, is recognized as the perfect biomaterial for forming strong, continuous networks and functional films for food packaging due to its outstanding film-forming capabilities, abundant sources, and biodegradability [11,12]. However, some drawbacks of pure CS film, including poor mechanical properties and low UV barrier performance, need be resolved to meet the expected performance in practical applications [13]. Many researchers have tried to enhance the performance of CS films by blending CS with other polysaccharides. For example, Jiang et al. used a blend film of CS/guar gum, have shown that the mechanical and functional properties of CS film could be improved by forming intermolecular hydrogen bond between guar gum and CS [10]. CS/guar gum films exhibit good homogeneity, compatibility, and transparency [10].

Locust bean gum (LBG), a biomacromolecule, is a natural polysaccharide extracted from the endosperm portion of carob seeds (Ceratonia siliqua) [14]. The physicochemical properties of LBG, such as appropriate biodegradability and outstanding biocompatibility, and low-cost extraction, make it suitable for food and pharmaceutical applications. LBG has proper film-forming attributes along with strong mechanical properties [15]. Consequently, blending LBG with CS may lead to the formation of a three-dimensional network structure through the interaction of hydrogen bonds between CS and LBG, potentially enhancing the physical properties of CS films. However, despite the potential benefits, there are limitations regarding the biological activity and functionality of various polymer blend films. In addition, the food packaging faces a more severe ordeal than the food itself; thus, CS/LBG film used as food packaging material requires not only enhanced antioxidant but also antibacterial activities and overall functionality [16].

Koelreuteria paniculata Laxm. (KPL), described as “money shaking tree” or “lantern tree”, is a tree or deciduous shrub belonging to the Sapindaceae family. Its fruits are bladder-shaped, triangular, three-celled capsules. Previous work has confirmed that KPL bracts contain polyphenolic compounds with good antioxidant and antimicrobial activities [17]. The KPL bracts extract (KBE) is rich in anthocyanins and other polyphenols. Anthocyanins are easily soluble in water and polar solvents, such as dilute acid, alcohols, dilute alkali. A characteristic feature of plant tissue is that their color varies in response to pH levels [18]. The pH-based sensing indicators can detect the accumulation of volatile amines during meat spoilage, causing significant color changes in pH-sensitive dyes loaded in polymer films [19]. However, KPL bracts are generally discarded or burned as garden waste, leading to resource wastage and contributing to environmental degradation. Therefore, KBE can serve as potential functional filler for the development of multi-functional active packaging films and high-value-added products. By utilizing KBE, the high-quality utilization rate of garden waste can be improved, reducing environmental pollution. The pH indicator film is a convenient and accurate method to provide qualitative information through visual colorimetric changes [20]. For example, Guo et al. used lychee shell extract as an indicator, making a composite film for food packaging to monitor fish freshness. Red color changed to light gray, indicating fish deterioration [18]. Therefore, the preparation of pH-indicating smart/active film substrates combined with KBE is a feasible method. To the best of our knowledge, no documentation exists regarding the incorporation of KBE into composite films for intelligent or active packaging. Despite the extraction process yielding valuable compounds, a considerable volume of waste residue is generated, which has high potential for utilization as a carbon source.

Presently, carbon dots (CDs) have attracted considerable attention as a newly emerging carbon-based material [21]. CDs can be synthesized from various carbon sources through hydrothermal reactions. These nanoparticles offer several advantages, including small size, adjustable functional groups, strong antibacterial/antioxidant activity, rich surface functional groups, and excellent water solubility, making them promising for applications in the packaging industry [21]. The photocatalytic process is complex; some studies have shown that CDs has strong antibacterial activity only under the xenon lamp illumination [22]. In addition, metal doping can improve the antibacterial activity of CDs, enabling strong antibacterial effects without photocatalytic condition [23]. However, some studies have shown that the antibacterial activity of CDs relies on photodynamic effects, where the photosensitizer is stimulated by visible light, triggering biochemical reactions that inactivate microbes [22]. In addition, without a good stabilizer, the formed nanoparticles tend to aggregate, reducing their antimicrobial and antioxidant properties. In this regard, stable and functional CDs can be achieved by doping metal nanoparticles. Therefore, doping CDs with TiO2 is a good strategy to obtain stable CDs with excellent functional properties [24]. The addition of Ti-CD to polymer matrix can fill the voids in the film, making the network structure of the film more dense and thus improving the antibacterial, antioxidant, water resistance, and other properties of the film. However, no reports have yet explored the structure and functionality of Ti-CDs synthesized from the filter residue after extracting active ingredients from KPL bracts. Additionally, the effects of KBE and Ti-CDs as active fillers on the structure and functionality of CS/LBG (CG)-based films require further investigation.

SWOT analysis indicates that smart packaging films offer advantages such as technological innovation, enhanced safety, and environmental benefits. However, they face challenges such as high costs, insufficient technological maturity, and low consumer awareness. Current market opportunities lie in increasing demand, technological innovation, and policy support. External threats stem from market competition, rapid technological updates, and regulatory changes. To ensure competitiveness, it is necessary to focus on innovation, promotion, and attention to market regulations. Therefore, this study aimed to utilize CG as the film-forming matrix, incorporating KBE and bio-waste (leftover after KBE extraction) derived Ti-CDs to construct multifunctional green food packaging films. The composition and performance of these films were thoroughly investigated. The present study investigated the structure and function of KBE and Ti-CDs and their impacts on the microstructure, barrier possessions, mechanical possessions, pH sensitivity, antioxidant effects, antibacterial properties, and biodegradability of the prepared films. In summary, to our knowledge, the novelty of this research lies in the development of the first multifunctional sustainable film using KBE and Ti-CDs derived from biological waste. This innovation significantly impacts the utilization of agricultural waste for high-value applications, the diverse development, and the application of biodegradable food packaging.

KPL was collected from the campus of Anhui Agricultural University. Chitosan (100–200 mpa·s, degree of deacetylation ≥95 %), 2,2′-Azino-bis (3-ethylbenzothiazo line-6-sulfonic acid) diammonium salt (ABTS) and 2,2-diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl(DPPH), glycerol (MW = 92.09, purity ≥99 %) and Titanium dioxide (rutile, 40 nm) were bought from Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). LBG was bought from Shanghai yuanye Bio-Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Shrimp was obtained from local Fresnillo (Hefei, China). Polyethylene (PE) cling film was purchased from Nanya plastic industry Co., Ltd. (Nanchang, China). All other reagents and chemicals were analytical grade. Specific material data and safety data sheets are shown in Table S1 (Supporting Information).

KBE extracted from KPL bracts following the process explained by Xie et al. [25]. KPL bracts were washed, freeze-dried, and grounded into a powder. After passing through a 60-mesh sieve, the powder (10 g) was placed in a 200 mL solution of ethanol (80 %, v/v) containing 1 % HCl (v/v) and set aside at 4 °C for an entire day. The solution was gently mixed every 2 h. After centrifuging for 10 min at 5000 rpm, the solution was passed through a filter paper. No other solvents were used in centrifugation and filtration process. The resultant extract subsequently concentrated at 60 °C. Finally, the KBE was acquired through freeze-drying and stored at 4 °C until further use.

The total phenolic content (TPC) of KBE was determined using the Folin-Ciocalteu method. TPC was measured in milligrams of gallic acid (GA) per gram of extract, and each trial was conducted at least three times. Using DPPH and ABTS free radical scavenging assays, the antioxidant capacity of KBE was assessed [26]. In short, KBE solution (2 mL, 1 mg/mL) was added to ABTS+ solution (2 mL, 0.15 mM) at 734 nm for 15 min, and to DPPH solution (2 mL, 0.15 mM) at 517 nm for 30 min in order to measure absorbance. The pH differential method was used to determine the anthocyanin content of KBE [27]. The KBE solution was dissolved in a buffer solution with pH 1 and pH 4.5, absorbance of KBE solution (1 mg/mL) was measured at 520 nm and 700 nm.

Following the proper dilution, KBE solutions (1 mL, 1 mg/mL) were added to four different pH buffer solutions (varying from pH 3 to 11) for 3 min each. Photographs were taken to record the solution's color shift. Then, using a UV–visible spectrophotometer, the absorption spectra of the solutions in various buffer solutions were recorded (Lambda 35, PerkinElmer, America) from 450 to 700 nm.

After extracting KBE, the filter residue was dried and used as a carbon precursor for the synthesis of CDs. Ti-CDs were manufactured base on the previously described process with some modifications [28]. Briefly, in 60 mL of distilled water, 1.0 g of KPL bract powder and 100 mg of TiO2 nanoparticles (10 % by weight of KPL bract powder) were mixed together. After gently stirring the mixture for 10 min, it was placed in a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated to 190 °C for 6 h. Following the reaction, the yellowish-brown solution was allowed to room temperature before being filtered through a Whatman filter with a pore size of 100 μm. The solution was then centrifuged for 30 min at 10,000 rpm. The centrifugation for 30 min at 10,000 rpm was to remove the unreacted KPL bract and TiO2. The residue was removed to further purify it to get the solution of TiO2-doped CDs (Ti-CDs). The supernatant was filtered using a Whatman membrane filter (nylon filter, 0.22 μm pore size, 25 mm diameter) to further purify Ti-CDs. The brown-yellow Ti-CDs solution was kept at 4 °C for further analysis.

Using an electron transmission microscope, the microscopic structure of the Ti-CDs was examined (TEM, HT7700, Hitachi Production Co., Ltd., Japan) [10]. The absorption and fluorescence responses were measured utilizing an ultraviolet-visible spectrophotometer and a fluorescence spectrophotometer (F-2710, HITACHI, Japan), respectively. The manufactured CDs' elemental composition and functional groups were examined using Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy in the 4000–500 cm−1 range (Nicolet iS50, Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were obtained using an X-ray diffractometer (D8 Advance, Bruker, Germany). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was conducted using a K-alpha X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Panalytical Empyrean, PANalytical B.V., Holland) to analyze elemental composition.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Functional properties of CDs and Ti-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Employing DPPH and ABTS techniques, the antioxidant efficacy of CDs and Ti-CDs at various concentrations (12.5, 25, 50, 75, and 100 μg/mL) was investigated [25]. With a fixed amount of CDs and Ti-CDs solution, a mixture of DPPH and ABTS test solution (10 mL) was prepared, and the resulting mixture was allowed to remain at room temperature for 30 min.

Absorbance measurements were carried out at 517 nm and 734 nm to determine the antioxidant capability. The antimicrobial activity of CDs and Ti-CDs was assessed using the agar well diffusion method against food-borne pathogenic bacteria (S. aureus and E. coli). An inoculum of 6 Log CFU/mL of the test bacteria was added to the agar plate's surface using a sterile brush. Holes with an 8 mm diameter were punched into the agar, and 100 μL of CDs and Ti-CDs were injected. The plates were incubated at 37 °C for 24 h, and the diameter of the inhibition zones was measured.

Composite films based on CG were fabricated following the approach outlined by Tang et al. [29], with slight adjustments. 1 g of CS (2 %, w/v) was liquefied in a 1 % acetic acid solution (50 mL), mechanically stirred for 30 min at 80 °C, and 1 g of LBG (2 %, w/v) was dissolved in distilled water for stirred 30 min and at 80 °C (50 mL). The polymer solutions were dissolved in a 1:1 ratio (v/v), and continuously stirred at 40 °C. Glycerol (30 % based on polymer mass) was added as a plasticizer. Different amounts of Ti-CDs (1, 2, and 3 wt%) were added and vigorously stirred at 40 °C for 1 h. In our experiments, we found that films containing 3 wt% Ti-CD had the most comprehensive performance. Therefore, the subsequent composite films were prepared by 3 wt% Ti-CD. Afterwards, 5 wt% extracts were added into the 3 wt% Ti-CDs solution and stirred for 30 min. Film-forming solution was degassed through ultra-sound, and 100 mL solution poured into a 15 × 20 cm mold, then allowed to dry for 6 h at 40 °C. Before testing, all the films were removed from the mold and store for 2 days at 25 °C and 75 % relative humidity. The films with varying Ti-CD compositions (0–3 wt%) were referred to as CG, CG-1%CDs, CG-2%CDs and CG-3%CDs, while CG and CG-3%CDs with KBE were named CG-KBE and CG-3%CDs-KBE, respectively. Fig. 1 provides a straightforward explanation of the composite film preparation procedure.

Scanning electron microscope (S-4800, Hitachi Production Co., Ltd., Japan) was utilized to document the morphology of the materials' surfaces and cross sections. Energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS, Ametek, US) was used to evaluate the films' surface elements [18].

FT-IR spectrometer (Nicolet iS50, Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) was utilized in range of 4000–500 cm−1 at a magnification of 4 cm−1 with 32 scans to observe the intermolecular effects among various film elements [10].

Cu Kα radiation was utilized to test films of XRD patterns between 2θ = 5° and 90° using an X-ray diffractometer. The crystallinity of the films was calculated via the following formula:

For thermal properties, thermogravimetric analyzer (SDT 650, TA, USA) was employed. Films samples were heated in N2 (20 mL/min) from 30 °C to 600 °C at a speed of 10 °C/min.

The optical properties of the film were evaluated using the method of Li et al. [19], with slight adjustments. Transmittance of films was measured using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer. Digital cameras were used to record the appearance of film samples that were placed on a piece of white paper with the school's emblem. A colorimeter (PS2020, Sanenshi Intelligent Technology Co., Ltd., China) was used to measure the films' color parameters (L*, b*, a*, and ΔE) [10]. Film samples were then immersed in a range of buffer solutions (pH 3–11), and any color changes were captured on camera to evaluate pH sensitivity. Ammonia-sensitivity of film was determined by placing the films (20 × 20 mm) in a flask containing ammonia solution, and the resulting color variations were captured on camera.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thickness, moisture content (MC) and water solubility (WS)
ce:section.ce:para: The thickness of the films was measured using a thickness meter. After drying films for 24 h at 105 °C, the MC was calculated as the percentage weight loss of films to their initial weight [10]. After drying to a consistent weight, 2 × 2 cm films were agitated in water for 6 h at 110 rpm. WS was calculated as the ratio of the dry content to the initial weight after dispersion in water. All experiments were performed in triplicate.

The mechanical properties of the film were evaluated using the method of Li et al. [19]. Tensile strength (TS) and extension at break (EAB) were measured using a universal testing machine (XLW (PC)-500N, Languang Electromechanical Technology Co., Ltd., China). The films were stored for 20 days at 4 °C and the TS and EAB were tested during storage.

This WVP of the films was estimated by the modified ASTM E96 (1996) method [30]. In short, 40 mm diameter circular film specimens were seed sealed with melted wax over a 30 mm deep cup filled with 12 g of anhydrous CaCl2 to maintain 0 % relative humidity inside film. Next, the cups are put in an evaporator with a saturated NaCl solution to maintain 75 % relative humidity outside the film. The cups are weighed every 24 h for 7 days.

The samples were tested for OP based on previous studies [27]. Tubes sealed with various films each contained 4 mL of linoleic acid and were stored in the dark at 23 ± 3 °C. The weight of tubes was monitored over a period of 7 days.

The WCA of the film were evaluated using the method of Li et al. [19]. Contact angle goniometer (SDC-200S, Sunding Precision Instruments Co., Ltd., Dongguan, Guangdong, China) was used to measure the films' water contact angle (WCA). A high-speed digital camera captured an image of a 5 μL drop of distilled water that was placed on the film's surface. WCA measurements were taken on the film surface at six different spots.

To assess antioxidant activity, 20 mg of film was submerged in a DPPH ethanol solution (0.2 mM, 4 mL) in a dark tube for 30 min, and absorbance was measured at 517 nm [31]. After 10 min of immersion of 80 mg of film sample into ABTS+ solution (8 mL), absorbance was measured at 734 nm [27].

The antimicrobial capacity of the films was measuring using the zone of depression method [31]. Briefly, the film samples were divided into 8 mm discs, placed in Petri dishes coated with E. coli and S. aureus, and incubated at 37 °C for 24 h in an incubator with a steady temperature. After incubation, images were captured with a digital camera, and the diameter of the inhibition zones was measured.

The degradation test in soil was evaluated using the method of Nandi et al. [32], with slight adjustments. The sample films were divided into 30 × 30 mm sheets and buried in natural soil at a depth of 5 to 10 cm. Polyethylene (PE) was used as a control. The film samples were removed from the soil every 7 days to record their degradation and evaluate their biodegradability. The average soil temperature and humidity were approximately 25 °C and 35 %, respectively.

The film was used to detect the freshness of shrimp as reported by Dong et al. [33]. CG-3%CDs-KBE was selected in subsequent experiments due to its better comprehensive performance. The film was divided into 1 × 1 cm pieces as an indicator to keep track of the shrimp's freshness. After adding the shrimp sample to the petri dish and covering it with film, it was stored at 4 °C or room temperature. A pH meter was used to measure the pH of the mixture after two grams of minced shrimp were combined with twenty milliliters of deionized water. Shrimp was regularly weighed during storage to calculate weight loss. The color parameters (L*, b*, a* and ∆E) of the films during storage were measured by colorimeter. Total volatile basic nitrogen (TVB-N) in shrimp was measured using an automatic Kjeldahl analyzer (KN520, Alva, China).

The film-forming solutions were applied to strawberries by dip coating method to evaluate the preservation ability of the film [34]. The strawberries were applied with following treatments: water, CG, CG-3%CDs, CG-KBE and CG-3%CDs-KBE. All treated strawberries were stored at room temperature (25 °C) with 50 % relative humidity and analyzed at an interval of 2 days for up to 10 days of storage. Photos of the appearance of strawberries were taken at predetermined intervals. Weight loss was calculated by weighing strawberries at the 0 day and at each storage interval. A texture analyzer (TA-XT plus, Stable Micro systems, UK) was used to measure the hardness.

According to method reported by Guo et al. [34], the rotting rate of strawberries was expressed as the ratio of the number of decayed strawberries to the total number of initial strawberries. Microbiological analysis was carried out according to the method reported by Li et al. [35]. Concisely, strawberries (25 g) were squeezed for juice, mixed with 225 mL sterile saline solution using a blender, and a series of dilutions were prepared. 1 mL of each dilution was pipetted into the aseptic petri dish, followed by adding approximately 15 mL plate count agar. The samples were evenly distributed by rotating the petri dish. After that, the solidified plates were incubated at 37 °C for 48 h. Each experiment was performed in triplicate and the total viable counts (TVC) were expressed as log CFU/ g.

The biosafety evaluation of the films was evaluated using the method of Qin et al., with slight adjustments [36].The strawberries coating with CG-3%CDs-KBE were washed with pure water to get surface layer removed. The washing solution was collected and used to cultivate the bean sprouts. The water-cultured bean sprouts served as control group. After 0, 5, and 10 days of watering, the condition of the plants and CG-3%CDs-KBE treatment was photographed. The growth parameters, including average plant height and average germination rate, were calculated. The biosafety of CG-3%CDs-KBE was evaluated by recording the growth status, plant height and germination rate of bean sprouts.

All experiments were repeated at least three times. All data were subjected to one-way analysis of variance and Duncan's multiple comparison test (significance level p < 0.05) to verify whether there were significant differences among means. All figures were obtained using Origin 2021 software. Experimental data are expressed as the mean ± standard deviation (SD).

KBE has total anthocyanins of 20.8 ± 2.59 mg/g and total phenolics of 1037 ± 2.14 mg/g, indicating its high biological activity due to abundance of phenolic compounds. The free radical scavenging ability of KBE was 89.60 ± 0.85 % for DPPH and 93.20 ± 0.79 % for ABTS, demonstrating its significant antioxidant potential, making it suitable for developing active packaging. Color variations of KBE under different pH (3−11) environments are shown in Fig. 2a. The anthocyanins in KBE changed their structure in different acidic and alkaline environments and showing different colors [37]. At pH 3, KBE turned dark red, gradually lightening as acidity decreased (from pH 4 to pH 6). At pH 7, it became light brown, and in neutral and alkaline conditions, it turned brown. As the solution's pH increased, the color of the solution grew deeper, from a brownish yellow at pH 8 to a dark brown at pH 12. KBE discoloration is primarily attributed to anthocyanins, which undergo color changes in response to variations in acidity and alkalinity. In general, anthocyanins exist mainly in the bright red flavin cations form in highly acidic media. As the pH approaches neutral, anthocyanins predominantly form carbinol pseudo-bases or chalcones, causing the red hue to lighten. The primary form of anthocyanins in high alkaline media are quinone groups; when the alkalinity of the solution increased, the color of solution changed from colorless to blue and from light to dark [18]. Different structural characteristics of anthocyanins provide various colors or mechanisms of color change.

The UV–vis absorption spectrum of KBE in various buffered solutions (pH 3–11) is displayed in Fig. 2a. When the pH value is constantly changing, the absorption peak of KBE solution shifts significantly. When the pH levels of the KBE mixture were ≤5, an absorption peak appeared at 520–540 nm, and at pH > 5, an elevated absorption point appeared at 580–600 nm. Similar shifts in the UV–vis absorption peak from 525 nm to 615 nm were observed by Huang et al. when studying pH fluctuations in cauliflower extract (from 2 to 13) [38].

TEM and particle size distribution images of materials are shown in Fig. 2b-c. TEM results confirm that the Ti-CDs were spherical, with size distribution in the quantum range (<2 nm) and were monodisperse, with particle diameters ranging from 0.20 to 1.80 nm. Additionally, the optical characteristics of Ti-CDs were investigated. The effective synthesis of Ti-CDs is confirmed by prominent peak values of absorption near 275 nm that are attributed to the n-π* transition (COH or CO bond), as seen in the UV–vis absorption spectra (Fig. 2d, black line). The synthesis of CD was confirmed by the blue fluorescence emitted by the Ti-CDs solution when exposed to 365 nm UV light (illustration: Fig. 2d). The carbon source and synthesis process of CDs determine their fluorescence intensity of emission under ultraviolet (UV) radiation [38]. At an optimal excitation wavelength of 370 nm, the highest fluorescence (FC) emission of Ti-CDs was seen at 448 nm (Fig. 2d). The FC spectrum shows that Ti-CDs have a standard wavelength of excitation association, where the FC emission peak redshifts as the excitation wavelength increases (Fig. 2e). Earlier research examined the variable FC emission properties of CDS due to different surface imperfections and the varied size of nanoparticles [38].

ce:section.ce:section-title: FT-IR, XRD and XPS analyses of Ti-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: FTIR spectroscopy (Fig. 2f) was performed on CDs, TiO2, and Ti-CDs to study the bonding composition and functional groups of the materials. All spectra show absorption peaks at 3423, 1644, and 1400 cm−1, attributed to the stretching vibrations of the OH and NH bonds, the bending vibrations of the hydroxyl group, and the skeleton vibrations of the symmetric carboxylate (CO) stretching, respectively [21]. Besides this, the vibration peaks of CN (1450 cm−1), COOH (1705 cm−1), NH (3220 cm−1) can also be observed in CDs [21], indicating the presence of amides and carboxyl groups on the surface of CDs. Compared with TiO2, Ti-CDs has some novel characteristics; the CO peak becomes stronger and COC peaks appear at 1242 cm−1 [39]. These characteristic peaks exist on CDs indicate the successful coupling of CDs with TiO2 [21]. Moreover, the peak of CO was enhanced at 1069 cm−1, and the 1448 cm−1 peaks indicated the TiOC bond formation, verifying the successful doping of carbon dots with TiO2 [24]. Fig. 2g shows the XRD patterns of TiO2, CDs and Ti-CDs. The curve of CDs and Ti-CDs exhibits a broad peak centered at 22.5°, which is attributed to (002) planes of graphitic carbon [21]. The peak position of synthetic Ti-CDs was similar to that of pure CDs, indicating that Ti doping does not change the crystal phase of CDs. To further investigate the chemical composition of Ti-CDs, XPS analysis was conducted. The complete investigated spectra obtained (Fig. 2h) showed the presence of nitrogen, carbon, oxygen, and titanium, indicated by the peaks positioned around ~398.0, 283.2, 530.5 and 460.0 eV, respectively. Additionally, the deconvolution high resolution C 1s spectrum of Ti-CDs (Fig. 6i), displayed three distinct peaks at ~288.1, 286.4 and 284.8 eV, corresponding to CO, CC/CC (graphite) and CO/CN, respectively [21]. The N 1s spectrum (Fig. 6j) exhibited three characteristic peaks at ~339.4, 400.4 and 402.5 eV were corresponding to CNC, NC3, and NH, respectively. The O 1s spectrum (Fig. 6k) exhibited four characteristic peaks at ~530.7, 531.8, 532.9 and 533.8 eV were corresponding to Metal-O, CO, COH and CO, respectively. Moreover, the Ti 2p spectrum (Fig. 6l) exhibited two characteristic peaks at ~452.9 and ~463.9 eV, corresponding to Ti 2p3/2 and Ti 2p1/2, respectively, further supporting the formation of TiOC bonds in Ti-CDs composites.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antioxidant and antimicrobial activity of CDs and Ti-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: DPPH solutions of ethanol and ABTS+ solution were treated with various amounts of CDs and Ti-CDs to perform antioxidant tests. Following the reaction, absorbance drops for DPPH and ABTS were seen at 517 and 734 nm, respectively. There was a concentration-related increase in the radical-scavenging ability of CDs and Ti-CDs against DPPH and ABTS (Fig. 3a–b). The antioxidant property of CDs and Ti-CDs was 16.2 ± 3.1 % and 25.3 ± 3.2 % for DPPH and 16.5 ± 2.8 % and 34.5 ± 2.9 % for ABTS at a concentration of 12.5 μg/mL. Fig. 3a–b shows that the level of CDs and Ti–CDs increased the antioxidant efficacy for both radicals, and DPPH scavenging activity (92.1 ± 2.6 % and 98.2 ± 2.8 %) was lower than ABTS scavenging activity (95.7 ± 2.5 % and 98.7 ± 2.7 %) at 100 μg/mL concentration. Previous studies have also observed similar results [40]. The free radical elimination action of CDs and Ti-CDs increases with the quantity of –COOH and –OH groups on their surface. The exchange of H from the –OH of CDs and Ti-CDs to ABTS-H and DPPH-H can lessen and quench the scavenging effect of DPPH and ABTS [41]. There were reports on the free radical neutralization capacity of CDs and its application as active filler in food packaging technology [40].

Agar well-diffusion was used to investigate the antibacterial activity of CDs and Ti-CDs towards S. aureus and E. coli. As shown in Fig. 3c–g, the inhibition bands of CDs on S. aureus and E. coli were 10.79 ± 0.54 mm and 9.81 ± 0.61 mm, respectively, while those of Ti-CDs on S. aureus and E. coli were 23.27 ± 0.65 mm and 20.48 ± 0.63 mm, respectively. The findings demonstrated that Ti-CDs and CDs could successfully prevent gram-positive and gram-negative microbes from reproducing. Nonetheless, Ti-CDs had a greater bacteriostatic effect than CDs, and they had more bacteriostatic action against S. aureus than E. coli. The possible reason is that Ti-CDs are more prone to electrostatic interaction with monolayer peptidoglycans, proteins and phospholipids produced by gram-positive bacteria, which causes biofilms to break down and the release of intracellular components, while the thicker bilayer peptidoglycans cell walls of Gram-negative bacteria make interaction with Ti-CDs more difficult [22]. Regarding the antibacterial effect of CDs, previous studies have reported that ultra-small CDs can easily pass through the cell wall of microorganisms, interfere with cell activity, and shifting oxygen atoms can produce singlet oxygen molecules (1O2) and the hydroxyl radical (OH), which may result in reactive oxygen species (ROS) and oxidative damage that compromises physiological functions and disturbs equilibria in cells [42]. The formation of Ti ions from Ti-CDs may also be involved in the anti-microbial impact. These ions interface with the cytoplasmic contents of microbial cells to form ROS, which can rupture the cell wall of bacteria and cause cell death [24].

Homogeneity, structure, and morphology of the film matrix are significant aspects influencing its physical characteristics [43]. The surface of the CG (Fig. 4A-a) was relatively compact and smooth, without obvious holes and bubbles, indicating high compatibility between CS and LBG. Compared with CG, the surface of the film becomes smooth with the increase of the concentration of Ti-CDs, indicating that CG and Ti-CDs have good biocompatibility, and Ti-CDs can be uniformly dispersed in film matrix. When Sul et al. combined banana CD peels with chitosan/gelatin films, they noticed a comparable result [40]. Compared with CG, after adding KBE, the film's surface becomes uniformly smooth, which is due to the plant extract interacting with the biopolymer as a crosslinking agent and developing a network of cross-links in the polymer matrix [10]. Moreover, the surface of the film containing both the Ti-CDs and KBE (CG-3%CDs-KBE) remains smooth and dense.

The cross-section of films was examined to offer more detail on the impact of Ti-CDs and KBE on the morphology of the combined films (Fig. 4B). The cross-section of CG was rough with many wrinkles and an uneven internal texture. However, with the increase of Ti-CDs contents, the wrinkles gradually disappeared. Since Schiff base bonds and H–bonds were formed between the film matrix and Ti-CDs, its cross-section became flat, smooth, and compact, indicating a modification in their internal structure. Because of the H–bond between KBE and the film matrix, which raises the density of its internal framework, the sectional area of CG-KBE was thicker than that of CG. We previously observed a similar phenomenon when adding walnut peel extract to CS/guar gum films [10]. Compared with CG-3%CDs and CG-KBE, the cross-section of CG-3%CD-KBE was denser, indicating that Ti-CDs and KBE synergistically improve the internal structure of the composite film.

As depicted in Fig. 5a, each generated film exhibited a large peak correlating to the OH group at about 3326 cm−1 [24]. For CG, the CO vibration was associated with the distinctive peak at 1644 cm−1, while the peak detected at 2925 cm−1 was attributed to CH bonds. Furthermore, the CN bond vibrations were ascribed to the peak at 1412 cm−1, while the CO bond vibrations were attributed to the peak at 1039 cm−1 [27]. With the integration of Ti-CDs, the peak seen in the film samples at 3326 cm−1 (CG) migrated to 3345 cm−1 (CG-1%CDs), 3351 cm−1 (CG-2%CDs), and 3359 cm−1 (CG-3%CDs). These findings imply that Ti-CDs and the film matrix produced bonds [27]. The aldehyde group on Ti-CDs and an amino group of CS produced a CN bond through the Schiff base reaction as the level of Ti-CDs increased, and the peak around 1558 cm−1 strength has been significantly diminished [44]. The peak of CG at 3326 cm−1 was shifted to 3363 cm−1 (CG-KBE) and 3369 cm−1 (CG-3%CDs-KBE), attributed to the formation of hydrogen bonds. Furthermore, the NH bonding peak moved from 1412 cm−1 (CG) to 1403 cm−1 (CG-KBE) and 1401 cm−1 (CG-3%CDs-KBE) in the same direction. This indicates a possible interaction between CS's –OH/–NH2 groups and KBE's –OH groups [27]. In all films, the profiles were similar to CG, with no effect on the chemical composition of the polymer chain, except for a slight change in the position of the peaks due to the added Ti-CDs and KBE. The uniform binding and good compatibility of Ti-CDs, KBE, and the film matrix were confirmed.

Fig. 5b shows the samples' XRD patterns. The hydrated semi-crystalline configuration of CS contributed for the amorphous peak seen in CG at 22.3° [27]. The crystallinity of the films samples was calculated via Origin 2021 software. The results revealed that the crystallinities of CG, CG-1%CDs, CG-2%CDs, CG-3%CDs, CG-KBE and CG-3%CDs-KBE were 22.76 %, 26.34 %, 27.91 %, 29.42 %, 27.39 % and 33.71 %, respectively. With the increase of Ti-CDs content, the intensity of diffraction peak increased slightly because the interaction between Ti-CDs and the bonding between the initial polymers was improved by the film matrix. In addition, the disturbance of the contact between the initial polymers induced by the H-bond between CS and KBE weakened the peak for CG-KBE at approximately 23.6°. A comparable effect was seen when the rind of watermelon extract was mixed with the CS/guar gum film [31]. The use of KBE did not appreciably alter the XRD profile of CG-3%CDs, suggesting that the integrity of the film matrix network was not damaged.

Fig. 5c displays the outcomes of the Whole Genome Analysis (TGA) method. The film's substantial weight loss occurred in three stages. The initial stage (30–110 °C) was attributed to the volatilization of bonded moisture and acetic acid molecules [25]. The second stage (110–250 °C) involved the thermal decomposition of chemisorbed water, including H-bonds, as well as the disintegration of CS's amino groups, organic acids, and other less volatile substances [31]. The third stage (250–600 °C) was primarily due to the breakdown of CG and other KBE and Ti-CD components [27]. The residual dry material proportions at 600 °C were 27.33 % for CG, 28.02 % for CG-1%CDs, 28.41 % for CG-2%CDs, 29.46 % for CG-3%CDs, 29.00 % for CG-KBE, and 29.74 % for CG-3%CDs-KBE. Among these, the CG-3%CDs-KBE films had the greatest amount of dry matter at 600 °C. The thermal resistance of the CG-3%CDs-KBE film has been enhanced by a mixture of Ti-CDs and KBE. The enhanced thermal resistance of the CG-3%CDs-KBE film can be attributed to the formation of Schiff base and H–bonds between Ti-CDs, KBE, and the film matrix, which improves the film's thermal stability through the development of cross-linking bonds [45].

Fig. 5d shows the transmittance of light of several films. The film has an adequate light transmission above 400 nm. However, at UV light below 400 nm, especially in the 200–290 nm range, the transmittance was low, indicating that CG has a certain anti-ultraviolet ability. As the quantity of Ti-CDs increased (0–3 wt%), light transmission steadily decreased, with UV-B rays (280–320 nm) almost completely blocked. The UV barrier property of the Ti-CDs-added CG composite film was due to the light scattering and absorption capacity of Ti-CDs [40]. The addition of KBE further decreased light transmission, likely due to the phenolic components in KBE absorbing certain UV–vis light [10]. It is noteworthy that the film's light transmission decreased significantly upon the simultaneous incorporation of Ti-CDs and KBE, indicating that Ti-CDs and KBE can synergistically improve the light blocking ability of the film. Therefore, to shield food from UV radiation, CG-3%CDs-KBE may be able to develop UV-protective materials suitable for replacing plastic packaging.

Notably, each film showed good transparency on printing paper, indicating that they may all be used in real-world scenarios (Fig. 6a). However, with increasing Ti-CDs content, a decrease in the brightness of the films was observed, consistent with the composite film-altered L* color variable. As shown in Table 1 , the color parameters (L*, a*, b* and ΔE) of the film were as follows: the variable shifts from black to white are represented by L*, from green to red by a*, and from blue to yellow by b*. The overall color variation is indicated by ΔE [10]. The L* value dropped from 90.14 ± 0.69 to 68.03 ± 0.92 with increasing Ti-CDs content, suggesting that the composite films' lightness decreased. Following the insertion of Ti-CDs, the composite film progressively turned green, as shown by a* value rising from −2.64 ± 0.12 to 3.09 ± 1.13. The b* value increased from 2.80 ± 2.67 to 30.53 ± 2.89, indicating that the composite film gradually turned yellow after the addition of Ti-CDs. ΔE increased from 9.16 ± 1.24 to 43.65 ± 0.68 with the increase of Ti-CDs concentration. Compared with the CG, L* value of CG-KBE decreased to 71.37 ± 0.97, and a*, b* and ΔE value of CG-KBE significantly increased to 1.83 ± 0.30, 21.94 ± 0.85 and 35.26 ± 2.06 (p < 0.05), respectively, which may be related to KBE's influence on the film's color. A similar case was reported by Jiang et al. when walnut green husk extract was added to chitosan/guar gum active packaging film [10]. Compared with the CG-3%CDs and CG-KBE, the addition of Ti-CDs and KBE (CG-3%CDs-KBE) reduced the L* value, and increased a*, b* and ΔE values, likely due to the inherent color of Ti-CDs and KBE. The color characteristics of the composite films correspond to their visual appearance.

To ensure a thorough evaluation of the films' potential uses, their responses to varying pH levels were meticulously investigated. Fig. 6b illustrates the color changes of the film samples when immersed in various buffer solutions (pH 3 to 11). CG and CG-3%CDs exhibited no discernible color changes across the pH spectrum. Conversely, the CG-KBE and CG-3%CDs-KBE films displayed a shift to a dark red hue at pH 3, transitioning to a light pink between pH 3–6, and ultimately settling into a brown hue under both neutral and alkaline conditions. As the pH levels increased, the color of KBE-containing films gradually deepened, progressing from a brownish yellow at pH 8 to a rich brown at pH 12. This phenomenon is attributed to the presence of anthocyanins in KBE. In contrast, the absence of KBE in CG and CG-3%CDs resulted in no discernible color changes in response to acidic or alkaline environments. The previous section on the color shifting of KBE in various buffer mixtures outlined the mechanism of anthocyanin discoloration, noting that different anthocyanins structures had different discoloration colors or mechanisms [18]. Consequently, the color change of the CG-KBE and CG-3%CDs-KBE at different pH levels may be employed as a visual indicator of food freshness. Moreover, the sensing ability of the film samples to ammonia was verified, as shown in Fig. 6c. As expected, no color change was observed in the CG and CG-3%CDs films within 60 min, while both CG-KBE and CG-3%CDs-KBE showed color change. During ammonia sensing, the CG and CG-3%CDs exhibited no significant color change, likely due to the absence of natural pigments. Conversely, CG-KBE and CG-3%CDs-KBE exhibited a significant color change from brown to brownish yellow to deep brown. These findings suggest that films containing KBE will produce color changes at different pH and have good color sensitivity in alkaline environments. Since high-protein foods produce total volatile base nitrogen (TVB-N) during spoilage, altering the pH of the environment, CG-3%CDs-KBE is suitable as an indicator packaging for monitoring the freshness of high-protein foods.

The calculated thickness of the CG film was measured at 0.088 ± 0.008 mm, as indicated in Table 2 . The density of the film dramatically enhanced (p < 0.05) with the addition of Ti-CDs and KBE. This increase in thickness is attributed to the higher amount of dry matter in the films. The CG matrix has been enriched with hydrophilic groups (e.g., –OH and –NH2), and thus the films had the highest MC value (22.85 ± 0.72 %) [27]. Moisture content (MC) of the films was dramatically (p < 0.05) reduced from 22.85 ± 0.72 % to 18.62 ± 1.21 % with the addition of KBE. This decline was ascribed to the formation of H-bonds between the film matrix and KBE, which lowers the interaction between CG and molecules of water [27]. In addition, the natural extracts contained high amounts of hydrophobic solids, such as flavonoids and β-carotene, which are less likely to bind with water molecules [31]. When Ti-CDs were additionally added, film had a substantial (p < 0.05) drop in MC from 22.85 ± 0.72 % to 18.57 ± 1.01 %, which was caused by the hydrogen bonding between Ti-CDs and CG, which reduces the affinity of film matrix for water molecules [43]. The lowest MC values of the films were observed when both KBE and Ti-CDs were added. This is due to the synergistic effect of KBE and Ti-CDs on the film matrix.WS reflected the affinity of the films for water molecules. With the inclusion of KBE, the WS of the films considerably (p < 0.05) decreased from 30.73 ± 1.72 % to 28.12 ± 1.32 %. This reduction was due to the synthesis of H–bonds between KBE and the film substrate, which decreased the attraction of the water molecule. The WS of CG-CDs lowered from 28.81 ± 0.20 % to 25.55 ± 0.51 % as the content of Ti-CDs (1–3 %) increased. Ti-CDs and the film matrix strongly interacted to form a dense 3-D network pattern, reducing the solubility of the film and improving its stability in water. The solubility of the composite films was minimized when both CDs and KBE were present in the film matrix, as this combination exhibited the greatest interaction with the film matrix, resulting in a synergistic enhancement of the film's water resistance.

TS and EAB reflect important mechanical properties of biopolymer films [44]. Table 2 showed that CG has the lowest TS (14.93 MPa). As Ti-CD levels rise (from 1 % to 3 %), the TS value of CG-CD films increased from 19.62 MPa to 22.54 MPa. This rise in TS value is attributed to intra- and intermolecular hydrogen bonding between functional groups of Ti-CDs and the hydroxyl group of the base polymer, resulting in the nano-filler effect and increased energy required for fracture [24]. Compared to CG, the TS of CG-KBE significantly increased due to the crosslinking effect between KBE and the film matrix, but the TS was lower than that of CG-3%CDs. As expected, the composite film (CG-3%CDs-KBE) containing both Ti-CDs and KBE had the highest TS. However, the addition of Ti-CDs and KBE significantly (p < 0.05) reduced the EAB of the film, apparently due to the increased rigidity of the film reducing flexibility. Sul et al. also found a similar phenomenon where the addition of CDs to chitosan/gelatin film resulted in increased TS and decreased EAB [40]. Nevertheless, CG-3%CDs-KBE still exhibited good foldability (Fig. 7a). In addition, Fig. 7a demonstrates the favorable mechanical properties of CG-3%CDs-KBE and its capacity to sustain a 1 kg weight. However, PE film could not withstand a weight of 1 kg, indicating that the developed composite film containing Ti-CDs and KBE has the potential to replace plastic materials. After 20 days of storage, CG films showed the most significant decrease in TS and EAB, while the mechanical properties of other films remained high (Table S2 in Supporting Information). This was due to the high antioxidant and ROS free radical scavenging abilities of KBE and Ti-CDs. The scavenging of ROS free radicals is attributed to hydrogen donor carbon nuclei, functional surface groups, and other introduced reaction sites [46], which improves the oxidation resistance of the film and reduces the decline in physical properties during storage.

ce:section.ce:section-title: WVP, OP, and WCA of films
ce:section.ce:para: WVP is crucial for preventing vaporized water from transferring from food to the environment, which is essential for food preservation [47]. WVP represents the water vapor barrier capacity of films, as shown in Fig. 7b. The peak WVP of CG film is 5.98 × 10−11 (g·Pa−1·s·−1·m−1), while the WVP of the film containing Ti-CDs (1 %–3 %) decreases from 5.66 × 10−11 (g·Pa−1·s·−1·m−1) to 4.53 × 10−11 (g·Pa−1·s·−1·m−1). This reduction can be explained by the added Ti-CDs' ability to fill the gaps between the chains to create a dense network structure and increase tortuosity, which prevents water droplets from diffusing and improves the films' capacity to serve as a water vapor barrier [24]. The WVP of the CG-KBE was reduced to 4.09 × 10−11 (g·Pa−1·s·−1·m−1). The tortuosity of water vapor transport may be raised by the H–bond between KBE and the matrix of the film and by the dense structuring of the film that results from packed internal networks between KBE and the film matrix [27]. Similarly, Wang et al. also found that the incorporation of the rind of watermelon extract decreased the WVP value of CS/guar gum films [31]. When both Ti-CDs and KBE were present in the film matrix, the WVP was notably (p < 0.05) lowered to 3.77 × 10−11 (g·Pa−1·s·−1·m−1), due to the strong interactions between the Ti-CDs, KBE, and the film matrix, which synergistically enhanced water vapor barrier effect of films.

OP, shown in Fig. 7c, is an important indicator for assessing the films' barrier characteristics. By increasing Ti-CDs concentration (1 %–3 %), the OP value of composite films gradually decreased, attributed to the Ti-CDs increasing the internal content of the film matrix, complicating the path of oxygen. Compared to CG, the OP of CG-KBE was significantly reduced due to the increased density from the internal crosslinked pattern and the need for oxygen to travel a more convoluted path [10]. In addition, KBE's polyphenols partially consume oxygen, though the OP was still higher than that of CG-3%CDs [27]. As expected, the composite film (CG-3%CDs-KBE) containing both Ti-CDs and KBE had the lowest OP of 0.22 g/d·m2.

Water Contact Angle (WCA) is a significant indicator for assessing the hydrophobic properties of film surfaces. A higher WCA indicates stronger hydrophobicity [48]. WCA measurements are displayed in Fig. 7d. The WCA of CG possess 75.51 ± 2.01°, the WCA value of composite films gradually increased form 81.05 ± 2.12° to 89.23 ± 1.86°, with increasing concentration of Ti-CDs (1 %–3 %). The impact was attributed to the Ti-CDs' comparatively uniform distribution across the films, which promotes hydrogen bonding between Ti-CDs and biopolymers, reducing the level of free hydrophilic molecules in the films [47]. The inclusion of KBE caused a noteworthy rise in WCA (p < 0.05), influenced by the polar groups in KBE. As expected, the composite film (CG-3%CDs-KBE) containing both Ti-CDs and KBE had the highest WCA, indicating that CDs and plant extracts could synergistically improve the hydrophobicity of biopolymer films.

Based on the results shown in Fig. 7e–f, the CG film showed low antioxidant activity, scavenging 28.5 ± 2.3 % of DPPH radicals and 55.5 ± 2.2 % of ABTS radicals. However, with increasing Ti-CDs concentration (1 %–3 %), the antioxidant properties of film improved considerably (p < 0.05). In addition, the films' ABTS and DPPH radical-scavenger activities improved from 64.5 ± 3.1 % to 94.5 ± 2.5 % and 31.8 ± 3.1 % to 92.1 ± 2.5 %, respectively. The abundance of various reducing groups that donate electrons (OH, OCOH) on the surface of Ti-CDs may be liable for such modifications [47]. Such groups have a high capacity to donate hydrogen and can react with reactive species, such as fatty acid-free radicals, converting them into more stable products. This process breaks the vicious cycle of radicals that occur and prevents oxidative damage. The considerable level of polyphenols in the KBE extract caused a substantial (p < 0.05) rise in the antioxidant activity of the films. Polyphenols can capture free radicals, thereby preventing their propagation [49]. As expected, the composite film (CG-3%CDs-KBE) containing both Ti-CDs and KBE had the highest antioxidant properties due to the combined antioxidant capacities of these components.

The antibacterial efficacy of the films is presented in Fig. 7g–i, revealing notable differences in their performance. Specifically, among all tested samples, CG exhibited the lowest antibacterial activity. In contrast, CDs demonstrated significant inhibitory effects against both S. aureus and E. coli, with inhibition zones measuring 7.81 ± 0.81 mm and 8.24 ± 0.94 mm, respectively. The antibacterial effect of CG was mainly due to the cationic nature of CS, which interacts with food-borne cell membranes, whereas the amino group of CS interacts with negatively charged bacterial membranes, leading to the leakage of proteins and other intracellular material components of microorganisms [50]. With the addition of Ti-CDs (1 %–3 %), the antibacterial activity of the composite films was significantly enhanced (p < 0.05). The inhibition band of E. coli increased form 10.13 ± 1.23 mm to 15.61 ± 0.92 mm, and the inhibition band of the S. aureus increased form 11.35 ± 1.12 mm to 17.22 ± 1.33 mm. This phenomenon could potentially be attributed to the antimicrobial activity of the Schiff base formed during the reaction between Ti-CDs and CS, as reported by others [51]. Compared with CG, the inhibition bands of CG-KBE on S. aureus and E. coli were increased to 10.79 ± 0.54 mm and 9.81 ± 0.61 mm, respectively. The high polyphenol content in KBE increases cell membrane permeability, causes cell deformation, and inhibits DNA and RNA synthesis [52]. Moreover, active KBE can interact proteins in bacterial cells to inhibit bacterial growth and reproduction [53]. As expected, the composite film (CG-3%CDs-KBE) containing both Ti-CDs and KBE had the strongest antibacterial ability. Riahi et al. observed similar results with anthocyanins and carrageenan-based films combined with TiO2-doped CDs extracted from sweet potato peels, which also showed significant antibacterial capacity [24].

Biodegradability is essential for sustainability of active packaging. To assess biodegradability in a natural environment, composite films were embedded in soil, with commercial PE plastic wrap serving as a control. The degradation process of the film was determined by observing the morphological changes for 21 days, and the results are shown in Fig. 7j. The color of CG, CG-CDs, CG-KBE, and CG-3%CDs-KBE samples changed by the 7th day, and the surface color deepened from the 7th day of burial, gradually wrinkling and roughness. By the 14th day, the biopolymer film significantly contracted and decreased in size. By the 21st day, the films had degraded significantly, losing their original structural integrity and shape, indicating a high degradation of the film by soil microorganisms [3]. However, the PE wrap showed no significant change in the softness, integrity, color and smoothness at the end of the 21st day. Therefore, the developed CG-based film can be degraded in soil and is an environmentally friendly biomaterial.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visual monitoring of raw shrimp freshness
ce:section.ce:para: Shrimp, a protein-rich food that is highly susceptible to microbial infection. TVB-N has been released from shrimp surfaces due to microbial colonization, that alters the shrimp pH, thus changing the color of the smart label [49]. The shifting color of film and the TVB-N, pH and weight loss of shrimp during storage are presented in Fig. 8 . After storage at room temperature for 1 day, from bright yellow to brownish yellow the appearance of CG-3%CDs-KBE alters (Fig. 8e), the TVB-N of increased from 5.28 ± 3.12 mg/100 g to 26.65 ± 4.53 mg/100 g, and the pH of shrimp increased from 7.05 ± 0.03 to 7.13 ± 0.02 (Fig. 8a). These results are attributed to the proliferation of microorganisms on the surface of the prawns breaking down proteins to produce TVB-N, which led to changes in pH on the surface of the prawns [24]. These results aligned with Dong et al., who stated that microbes caused the TVB-N of shrimp to increase from 7.20 mg/100 g to 39.08 mg/100 g during a single day of preservation at 20 °C [33]. For raw shrimp samples, the TVB-N scales are as follows: fresh for <12 mg/100 g, edible but slightly decomposed for 12–20 mg/100 g, borderline for 20–25 mg/100 g, and inedible and spoiling for >25 mg/100 g [33]. The TVB-N of shrimp exceeded the limit, indicating that the shrimp were no longer be useable for 1 day. After being kept at room temperature for 3 days, the shrimp's pH and TVB-N grew dramatically (p < 0.05), reaching 109.67 ± 5.25 mg/100 g and 7.49 ± 0.02, respectively, with a weight loss rate of 6.52 ± 0.54 % (Fig. 8c). This is due to the bacteria consuming the dry materials on the shrimp surface. The brown color of the smart label became more apparent to the naked eye (Fig. 8e).

When the shrimps were stored at 4 °C for 2 days, color of smart label turned to slightly brownish-yellow color, and the TVB-N content and pH were increased from 5.15 ± 3.54 mg/100 g and 7.05 ± 0.04 to 16.41 ± 4.22 mg/100 g and 7.12 ± 0.04, respectively (Fig. 8b). These changes indicate that the shrimp were still edible but slightly deteriorated. After storage at 4 °C for 4 days, the film was brownish yellow, TVB-N content and pH were increased to 86.53 ± 6.24 mg/100 g and 7.23 ± 0.04, respectively, and the weight loss reached 3.89 ± 0.45 % (Fig. 8d), confirming that shrimp samples had deteriorated. The TVB-N level and pH values of shrimp exhibited a strong correlation throughout storage under multiple circumstances, as illustrated in Fig. 8a–b. To sum up, the color shifting of CG-3%CDs-KBE during storage accurately reflect the quality of shrimp (from light yellow to dark brown). In addition, by associating the color change of the label with the freshness of shrimp (Fig. 8f), the color parameters (L*, b*, a* and ∆E) of the films (Table S3 in Supporting Information) during storage were measured by colorimeter. The L* value dropped, a* value increased, and the b* value showed an overall downward trend during shrimp spoilage. Generally, ΔE > 5 indicates that the color change is visible to the human eye [54], ΔE > 5 in all data, thus the color change could be observed by the naked eye. Consumers can easily distinguish the freshness of shrimp with their naked eyes. These findings suggest that CG-3%CDs-KBE has potential as a freshness indicator for meat in the future.

The appearance of strawberry samples under different coating treatments at room temperature during storage is illustrated in Fig. 9a. Initially, there were no significant differences in the appearance of strawberries among all groups on 0 day. However, as storage time increased, the strawberry samples gradually deteriorated. On the 10th day of storage, all the strawberries in the control group were moldy, had significantly shrunk. Similarly, most strawberries in other treatment groups showed signs of mold, although the proportion of moldy strawberries in CG-3%CDs-KBE group was the smallest. The strawberries coated with CG-3%CDs-KBE have almost retained their original size and red appearance. This preservation effect can be attributed to the superior barrier properties, antibacterial, and antioxidant activity of CG-3%CDs-KBE, effectively maintaining the physical appearance and quality of strawberries throughout the storage period.

The most significant indication of strawberry storage is weight loss. The weight loss of fruits is due to the evaporation of cellular respiration, water, organic acid loss, and polysaccharide decomposition [25]. Therefore, with increased storage time, the weight loss of strawberries showed a rapid increase trend (Fig. 9b). Strawberry samples in the control group lost the most weight on day 10, followed closely by the CG group. This was caused by the composite film solution's enhanced barrier characteristics. The CG-3%CDs coating group showed better performance than CG-KBE coating group in preventing weight loss of strawberries, consistent with previous comparisons of barrier properties (Fig. 7b–c). Strawberry samples treated with CG-3%CDs-KBE lost little weight on day 10. Improving optimal water vapor and oxygen-blocking traits, KBE reduces water and dry mass loss from cell respiration and stomatal transpiration [10]. Furthermore, CG-3%CDs-KBE has significant antibacterial properties (Fig. 7g-i), which may hinder the growth of bacteria and reduce sample weight loss [55].

Hardness is an important indicator to evaluate the taste of strawberries. The decrease in fruit hardness can be attributed to several factors, including the loss of cell swelling pressure, the activity of cell wall degrading enzymes, and starch degradation in immature pulp [25]. Therefore, as the storage time increases, the hardness of strawberry samples gradually declines. After 10 days of storage, the strawberry hardness for the different groups followed the order: CG-3%CDs-KBE > CG-3%CDs > CG-KBE > CG > Control (Fig. 9c). CG-3%CDs-KBE coating effectively improved its protective effect against loss of hardness in strawberries, possibly due to the antibacterial properties of CG-3%CDs-KBE that prevent bacteria from destroying the strawberry cell wall [25].

In the first 6 days, the rotting rate was lower in the CG-KBE and CG-3%CDs treatment groups (Fig. 9d), with no decay observed in the CG-3%CDs-KBE treatment group, indicating that the addition of KBE and Ti-CDs can effectively inhibit strawberry decay. In the control group, the rotting rate increased significantly with the extension of storage time. In the CG-3%CDs-KBE treated group, the rotting rate of strawberries remained low after ten days of storage. The change in the TVC of different samples during storage is shown Fig. 9e. The initial TVC of strawberries was about 1.51 log CFU/g, which gradually increased during storage for all the samples. Currently, 7.0 log CFU/g of the TVC is considered as a tolerance limit to evaluate microbial quality of fruits [35]. The TVC of control group increased to 8.76 log CFU/g on the 8th day, which exceeded the 7.0 log CFU/g. The increase of TVC in coated groups was lower than that in control group due to the antibacterial activity of the coating. After 10 days of storage, the TVC of strawberries for the different groups followed the order: Control > CG > CG-KBE > CG-3%CDs > CG-3%CDs-KBE (Fig. 9e). The CG-3%CDs-KBE group exhibited the slowest increase in TVC attributing to its superior barrier properties and antibacterial activity. On the 10th day, the TVC of CG-3%CDs-KBE group was 4.64 log CFU/g, reduced by 5.50 log CFU/g compared with control group on the same day.

The inferred mechanism diagram of CG-3%CDs-KBE coating delaying strawberry spoilage is shown in Fig. 9f. Two main causes of fruit spoilage were nutrient oxidation and microbial infection [25]. The CG-3%CDs-KBE coating delayed the spoilage of fresh strawberries mainly through two ways: (I) The physical properties of CG-3%CDs-KBE coating provide good UV barrier properties, effectively protecting food from UV radiation. Enhanced oxygen-blocking qualities also reduce the amount of oxygen contacting the strawberry surface, slowing cellular respiration and organic matter usage. (II) In terms of biological activity, fresh strawberries exposed to CG-3%CDs-KBE coating experienced less microbial damage. The CG-3%CDs-KBE coating shows good free radical removal capabilities, allowing it to remove oxidizing free radicals from strawberry sample surfaces to avoid food oxidation. For these causes, CG-3%CDs-KBE is likely to emerge as a new class of packaging for food materials.

The biosafety of CG-3%CDs-KBE was evaluated by investigating its effect on the growth of hydroponic bean sprouts seeds. As shown in Fig. 10a, both water treatment and CG-3%CDs-KBE treatment had no significant effect on bean color. In addition, the average plant height following water treatment (19.4 cm) was lower than that of CG-3%CDs-KBE treatment (21.4 cm) after 10 days of culture, indicating that the discharge water of CG-3%CDs-KBE coating positively influences plant growth, demonstrating good biosecurity. Generally, seed germination is a key stage affecting the quality of germination [36]. After 3 days, the germination rates of sprouts treated with water and CG-3%CDs-KBE were 94 % and 98 %, respectively, suggesting that CG-3%CDs-KBE treatment promotes the germination of bean sprouts. These results indicate that CG-3%CDs-KBE does not inhibit seed germination and plant growth, rather promotes plant development. Two factors contribute to this effect: (I) CDs promotes plant growth and development by improving photosynthesis and mineral element absorption [56]. (II) Titanium ions promote plant growth and development [57]. Overall, this study confirms the good biosafety of CG-3%CDs-KBE, indicating its potential application in agricultural production.

In the current study, multifunctional green food packaging films were prepared for the first time by doping KBE and bio-waste-derived Ti-CDs into CS/LBG matrix. The results of FT-IR and XRD revealed that the film matrix and Ti-CDs formed a dense network topology through Schiff-based crosslinking reaction and intermolecular hydrogen bonding, with KBE being fixed in the film by hydrogen bonding. Morphological analysis revealed that the incorporation of Ti-CDs and KBE resulted in a more homogeneous microstructure of the films, representing that Ti-CDs and KBE have good compatibility with the polymer matrix. The film demonstrated the best barrier properties and mechanical strength when the amount of Ti-CDs was 3 wt%. The incorporation of Ti-CDs and KBE in the film matrix enhanced the mechanical properties, barrier properties, and UV resistance of film. Due to the presence of large amounts of polyphenols in KBE, CG-3%CDs-KBE film exhibited strong antimicrobial and antioxidant abilities. In addition, CG-3%CDs-KBE coating extended the shelf life of strawberry. The CG-3%CDs-KBE film exhibited strong ammonia and pH sensitivity, and the color changes were visible to the unaided eye. Additionally, KBE—recovered from garden waste—is inexpensive and degradable, and CG-3%CDs-KBE could monitor the freshness of shrimp during storage. The CG-CDs-KBE films have promising qualities as multipurpose green food packaging materials due to their good physical properties, pH-responsive color indicators, antibacterial and antioxidant activity, and good biosafety. Similarly, CG-3%CDs-KBE film can undergo shrinkage degradation in the soil and has good biosafety. In conclusion, this study has shown promising application prospects for the development of film. Further investigation into the potential of these films for monitoring the freshness of other meats and their impact on the shelf life of meats is warranted. This will broaden the practical utilization of CG-3%CDs-KBE in the future.

The cost of developed films is shown in Table S4 (Supporting Information). Currently, multifunctional food packaging is lab-scaled and more expensive than PE (approximately 15.23 CNY/100 g). Cost reduction is necessary for broader commercial viability. Ideally, smart sensor costs should be <10 % of packaging cost, but they often exceed 50 %. For high-value foods, the added expense is more reasonable. In the future, smart sensors read by portable smart devices will become common, providing real-time food quality and condition information. Such materials are crucial in preventing food fraud and ensuring safety. The industrial application of anthocyanin-based indicator films is hindered by stability issues, necessitating enhancements in thermal and mechanical properties. The goal is to develop smart packaging that is reusable, recyclable, and eco-friendly. While the research is in its early stages, it promises to lead to a new generation of affordable, convenient, and sustainable packaging materials that will improve the health, safety and sustainability of food.

Mei Li: Writing – original draft, Software, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Yingzhu Liu: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Methodology. Yanyan Wang: Data curation. Tiantian Liu: Software. Ziao Li: Investigation. Longwei Jiang: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Project administration, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Conceptualization.
